title: Classic X-Back Outdoor Sofa
summary: A full-size outdoor sofa with five mitered X panels forming the back, built for cushions rather than bare-wood seating.
description:
Five identical X panels are the backbone of this build, and "identical" is the operative word — build all ten X halves first, then stack them on top of each other before assembling a single one, and square up any mismatch while the pieces are still loose. A quarter-inch difference between panels is invisible on its own but obvious once five of them stand in a row.

The back assembly is genuinely the long pole here — laying out every joint, checking square, and pocket-holing 1-1/2" stock together takes patience, but it's the step the source plan explicitly flags as worth slowing down for, and that's good advice: a back that's out of square telegraphs into every board attached to it afterward.

One small comfort trick worth keeping: set the back cleat very slightly lower than the front cleat so the seat slats recline a few degrees rather than sitting dead level. It's a small angle, but it's the difference between a bench and a sofa you actually want to sit in.
timeLabel: 10–15 hrs across a couple of sessions

materials.name: Unfinished wood curtain rod finials
materials.note: Decorative detail on the X ends.

cutList.note: Both ends cut at 30° off square, ends parallel.
Both ends cut at 30° off square, ends not parallel, longest point measurement.
Set the back cleat slightly lower than the front for a reclined seat.
Cut to fit; add more if your cushions are soft.

steps.title: Build ten X halves
steps.body: Nail the shorter angled 1x2 pieces to the longer 1x2 pieces to form ten identical X halves. Test-fit that a 1x2 slots correctly into the middle of each before moving on.

steps.title: Join the halves into five X panels
steps.body: Combine the ten halves into five complete Xs. Stack all five on top of each other and check they match before proceeding — this is the checkpoint that catches a mismatched panel while it's still cheap to fix.

steps.title: Add the X ends
steps.body: Attach the 2x2 X ends to all five X panels, using either pocket holes (set for 3/4" stock, 1-1/4" screws — it's fine if the pocket hangs slightly over the board edge) or 2-1/2" screws or nails and glue.

steps.title: Build the two end assemblies
steps.body: On two of the five X panels, attach 2x3 tops and bottoms with pocket holes, then attach the legs to these two ends. Finish each end by adding the 2x6 apron, either with pocket holes or 2-1/2" screws countersunk from the outside. Set both completed ends aside.

steps.title: Build the back
steps.body: Lay out all the back pieces and mark the joints. Drill pocket holes set for 1-1/2" stock and assemble with 2-1/2" pocket hole screws, checking for square as you go. This is the longest single step in the build — take the time to get it right, since everything else registers off it.

steps.title: Attach the back to the ends
steps.body: Attach the finished back to the two end assemblies with 2-1/2" screws and glue, adjusting for square as you go.

steps.title: Attach the front rail and cleats
steps.body: Attach the front apron to complete the frame. Add the seat cleats, setting the back cleat slightly lower than the front — a small recline makes a real difference in comfort.

steps.title: Lay the seat slats and finish
steps.body: Lay and screw down the seat slats, adding more than the base cut list calls for if your cushions are on the softer side. Sand everything, attach the curtain-rod finials to the X ends for the finishing decorative touch, and apply an exterior finish.

images.alt: An outdoor sofa with a back made of five mitered X panels
